99: ]);
100: 
101: /**
102:  * Resume / continue triggers.
103:  *
104:  * `/speckit:resume` and deep-research `resume` produce writes (iteration-NNN.md
105:  * and JSONL appends), so they require Gate 3 even though the surface name sounds
106:  * read-only.
107:  */
108: export const RESUME_TRIGGERS: readonly TriggerEntry[] = Object.freeze([
109:   { pattern: '/speckit:resume',     kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
110:   { pattern: '/deep:research', kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
111:   { pattern: 'speckit:deep-research',   kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
112:   { pattern: '/deep:review',   kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
113:   { pattern: 'speckit:deep-review',     kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
114:   { pattern: 'resume the packet',        kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
115:   { pattern: 'resume the phase folder',  kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
116:   { pattern: 'reconstruct continuity',   kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
117:   { pattern: ':auto',               kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume', note: 'Matches alongside a speckit or /deep: loop command.' },
118:   { pattern: 'deep-research',       kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
119:   { pattern: 'deep research',       kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
120:   { pattern: 'deep-review',         kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
121:   { pattern: 'deep review',         kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
122:   { pattern: 'deep-loop',           kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
123:   { pattern: 'research loop',       kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
124:   { pattern: 'review loop',         kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
125:   { pattern: 'iteration loop',      kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
126:   { pattern: 'research sweep',      kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
127:   { pattern: 'research cycle',      kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
128:   { pattern: 'research run',        kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
129:   { pattern: 'research wave',       kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
130:   { pattern: 'review wave',         kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
131:   { pattern: 'looped investigation',kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
132:   { pattern: 'keep iterating',      kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
133:   { pattern: 'autoresearch',        kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
134:   { pattern: 'convergence',         kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
135:   { pattern: 'resume iteration',    kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
136:   { pattern: 'resume deep research',kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
137:   { pattern: 'resume deep review',  kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
138:   { pattern: 'continue iteration',  kind: 'phrase', category: 'resume' },
139: ]);
140: 
141: /**
142:  * Read-only disqualifiers.
143:  *
144:  * These tokens, when present WITHOUT a corresponding file-write / memory-save /
145:  * resume trigger, mark the request as read-only. This prevents false positives
146:  * for prompts like "analyze the decomposition phase" or "review / audit / inspect
147:  * the code".
148:  *
149:  * Note: `analyze`, `decompose`, `phase` were previously positive triggers but
150:  * false-positived on read-only review prompts. They are intentionally omitted
151:  * from positive triggers; `analyze` is included here as a read-only disqualifier.
152:  */
153: export const READ_ONLY_DISQUALIFIERS: readonly TriggerEntry[] = Object.freeze([
154:   { pattern: 'review',  kind: 'token', category: 'read_only' },
155:   { pattern: 'audit',   kind: 'token', category: 'read_only' },
156:   { pattern: 'inspect', kind: 'token', category: 'read_only' },
157:   { pattern: 'analyze', kind: 'token', category: 'read_only' },
158:   { pattern: 'explain', kind: 'token', category: 'read_only' },
159: ]);
160: 
161: /** Full canonical vocabulary, exported for docs / linting. */
162: export const GATE_3_VOCABULARY = Object.freeze({
163:   fileWrite:           FILE_WRITE_TRIGGERS,
164:   memorySave:          MEMORY_SAVE_TRIGGERS,
165:   resume:              RESUME_TRIGGERS,
166:   readOnlyDisqualifier:READ_ONLY_DISQUALIFIERS,
167: });

169: // ---------------------------------------------------------------
170: // 3. NORMALIZATION
171: // ---------------------------------------------------------------
172: 
173: /**
174:  * Normalize a prompt for matching: lowercase and collapse whitespace, but
175:  * preserve `/` and `:` so command forms like `/speckit:resume` survive.
176:  */
177: export function normalizePrompt(prompt: string): string {
178:   return canonicalFold(prompt)
179:     .toLowerCase()
180:     .replace(/\s+/g, ' ')
181:     .trim();
182: }
183: 
184: /** Tokenize a normalized prompt into alphanumeric + `/` + `:` words. */
185: export function tokenizePrompt(normalized: string): string[] {
186:   return normalized.split(/[^a-z0-9:/_-]+/).filter(Boolean);
187: }

189: // ---------------------------------------------------------------
190: // 4. CORE CLASSIFIER

193: /** Check if a single trigger entry matches a normalized prompt. */
194: export function matchesEntry(entry: TriggerEntry, normalized: string, tokens: string[]): boolean {
195:   if (entry.kind === 'phrase') {
196:     if (entry.pattern === ':auto' && entry.category === 'resume') {
197:       return normalized.includes(':auto') && (normalized.includes('speckit') || normalized.includes('/deep:'));
198:     }
199:     return normalized.includes(entry.pattern);
200:   }
201:   // token match ŌĆö must appear as a standalone word
202:   return tokens.includes(entry.pattern);
203: }
204: 
205: function triggerIndex(entry: TriggerEntry, normalized: string): number {
206:   return normalized.indexOf(entry.pattern);
207: }

213: ): boolean {

215:     const readIndex = triggerIndex(readOnlyEntry, normalized);
216:     if (readIndex < 0) {
217:       continue;
218:     }

40: <!-- ANCHOR:overview -->
41: ## 1. OVERVIEW
42: 
43: The `spec_kit` command group manages the full development lifecycle around spec folders. Commands cover planning, implementation, deep-research, resumption, and end-to-end workflows.
44: 
45: Each command loads a YAML workflow from `assets/` and executes it step by step. Most commands support `:auto` and `:confirm` execution modes.
46: 
47: SEARCH ROUTING: semantic or concept code discovery routes to `code_graph_query + Grep`; structural questions about callers, imports, dependencies, outlines, symbols, or impact route to `code_graph_query`; exact literal text or regex checks stay on grep-style search. `session_bootstrap`, `session_resume`, and `/speckit:resume` reuse this same contract.
48: 
49: <!-- /ANCHOR:overview -->
50: 
51: ---
52: 
53: <!-- ANCHOR:commands -->
54: ## 2. COMMANDS
55: 
56: | Command | Invocation | Steps | Description |
57: |---------|------------|-------|-------------|
58: | **plan** | `/speckit:plan <description> [:auto\|:confirm] [:with-phases]` | 7 | Create spec folder and plan without implementation. `:with-phases` adds phase decomposition pre-workflow |
59: | **implement** | `/speckit:implement <spec-folder> [:auto\|:confirm]` | 9 | Execute pre-planned work (requires existing plan.md) |
60: | **deep-research** | `/deep:research <topic> [:auto\|:confirm\|:review\|:review:auto\|:review:confirm]` | iterative | Autonomous deep research loop with convergence detection |
61: | **deep-review** | `/deep:review <target> [:auto\|:confirm]` | iterative | Autonomous code review loop with severity-weighted findings |
62: | **resume** | `/speckit:resume [spec-folder] [:auto\|:confirm]` | varies | Resume or recover work on an existing spec folder |
63: | **plan --intake-only** | `/speckit:plan --intake-only [description] [:auto\|:confirm]` | intake-only | Standalone intake that publishes `spec.md`, `description.json`, and `graph-metadata.json` |
64: | **complete** | `/speckit:complete <description> [:auto\|:confirm] [:with-research] [:with-phases]` | 14+ | Full end-to-end workflow combining all phases. `:with-phases` adds phase decomposition pre-workflow |
65: 
66: ### Command Dependencies
67: 
68: | Command | Requires |
69: |---------|----------|
70: | `plan` | Nothing (creates new spec folder) |
71: | `implement` | Existing `plan.md` in spec folder |
72: | `deep-research` | Nothing (iterative research with convergence detection) |
73: | `deep-review` | Target files or spec folder to review |
74: | `resume` | Existing spec folder with saved state or recoverable session context |
75: | `complete` | Nothing (runs full lifecycle) |
76: 
77: <!-- /ANCHOR:commands -->
78: 
79: ---
80:

82: ## 3. STRUCTURE
83:

89: Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ plan.md           # /speckit:plan - Planning only (+ `--intake-only` standalone intake)
90: Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ resume.md         # /speckit:resume - Resume existing work
91: ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ assets/           # YAML workflow definitions

93:     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_complete_confirm.yaml

103: Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ review.md                 # /deep:review - Autonomous code review loop

105: ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ assets/

119: <!-- ANCHOR:workflow-progression -->
120: ## 4. WORKFLOW PROGRESSION
121: 
122: The typical development lifecycle follows this progression:
123: 
124: ```text
125: /deep:research (optional)
126:     |
127:     v
128: /speckit:plan (create spec folder + plan.md)
129:     |
130:     v
131: phase (optional: decompose into phase children)
132:     |
133:     v
134: /speckit:implement (execute plan.md tasks)
135:     |
136:     v
137: /speckit:resume (continue in a new or interrupted session)
138: ```
139: 
140: The `complete` command combines research, plan, and implement into a single invocation.
141: 
142: ### Agent Delegation
143: 
144: | Command | Delegates To |
145: |---------|-------------|
146: | plan | Main agent owns planning and may reuse the shared intake contract (`../../skills/system-spec-kit/references/workflows/intake_contract.md`); /deep:research optional |
147: | implement | @general (code changes), distributed governance for packet docs |
148: | deep-research | /deep:research (iterative investigation) |
149: | deep-review | /deep:review (iterative code audit) |
150: | resume | Loads memory context, continues from last state |
151: | phase | Main agent creates packet folders, @general runs scripts as needed |
152: | complete | /deep:research and @general as needed, with the shared intake contract (`../../skills/system-spec-kit/references/workflows/intake_contract.md`) when packet state requires repair |
153:

155:

---
title: "Spec Kit Commands"
description: "Slash commands for the spec folder development lifecycle including planning, implementation, deep-research, debugging, handover, resumption, and interrupted-session recovery."
trigger_phrases:
  - "spec kit command"

# Spec Kit Commands

> Slash commands for the spec folder development lifecycle from planning through completion.

---

<!-- ANCHOR:table-of-contents -->
## TABLE OF CONTENTS

- [1. OVERVIEW](#1--overview)
- [2. COMMANDS](#2--commands)
- [3. STRUCTURE](#3--structure)
- [4. WORKFLOW PROGRESSION](#4--workflow-progression)
- [5. EXECUTION MODES](#5--execution-modes)
- [6. USAGE EXAMPLES](#6--usage-examples)
- [7. FAQ](#7--faq)
- [8. TROUBLESHOOTING](#8--troubleshooting)
- [9. RELATED DOCUMENTS](#9--related-documents)

<!-- /ANCHOR:table-of-contents -->

---

<!-- ANCHOR:overview -->
## 1. OVERVIEW

The `spec_kit` command group manages the full development lifecycle around spec folders. Commands cover planning, implementation, deep-research, resumption, and end-to-end workflows.

Each command loads a YAML workflow from `assets/` and executes it step by step. Most commands support `:auto` and `:confirm` execution modes.

SEARCH ROUTING: semantic or concept code discovery routes to `code_graph_query + Grep`; structural questions about callers, imports, dependencies, outlines, symbols, or impact route to `code_graph_query`; exact literal text or regex checks stay on grep-style search. `session_bootstrap`, `session_resume`, and `/speckit:resume` reuse this same contract.

<!-- /ANCHOR:overview -->

---

<!-- ANCHOR:commands -->
## 2. COMMANDS

| Command | Invocation | Steps | Description |
|---------|------------|-------|-------------|
| **plan** | `/speckit:plan <description> [:auto\|:confirm] [:with-phases]` | 7 | Create spec folder and plan without implementation. `:with-phases` adds phase decomposition pre-workflow |
| **implement** | `/speckit:implement <spec-folder> [:auto\|:confirm]` | 9 | Execute pre-planned work (requires existing plan.md) |
| **deep-research** | `/deep:research <topic> [:auto\|:confirm\|:review\|:review:auto\|:review:confirm]` | iterative | Autonomous deep research loop with convergence detection |
| **deep-review** | `/deep:review <target> [:auto\|:confirm]` | iterative | Autonomous code review loop with severity-weighted findings |
| **resume** | `/speckit:resume [spec-folder] [:auto\|:confirm]` | varies | Resume or recover work on an existing spec folder |
| **plan --intake-only** | `/speckit:plan --intake-only [description] [:auto\|:confirm]` | intake-only | Standalone intake that publishes `spec.md`, `description.json`, and `graph-metadata.json` |
| **complete** | `/speckit:complete <description> [:auto\|:confirm] [:with-research] [:with-phases]` | 14+ | Full end-to-end workflow combining all phases. `:with-phases` adds phase decomposition pre-workflow |

### Command Dependencies

| Command | Requires |
|---------|----------|
| `plan` | Nothing (creates new spec folder) |
| `implement` | Existing `plan.md` in spec folder |
| `deep-research` | Nothing (iterative research with convergence detection) |
| `deep-review` | Target files or spec folder to review |
| `resume` | Existing spec folder with saved state or recoverable session context |
| `complete` | Nothing (runs full lifecycle) |

<!-- /ANCHOR:commands -->

---

<!-- ANCHOR:structure -->
## 3. STRUCTURE

```text
spec_kit/
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ README.txt        # This file, 6-command index and workflow guide
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ complete.md       # /speckit:complete - Full end-to-end workflow
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ implement.md      # /speckit:implement - Execute planned work
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ plan.md           # /speckit:plan - Planning only (+ `--intake-only` standalone intake)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ resume.md         # /speckit:resume - Resume existing work
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ assets/           # YAML workflow definitions
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_complete_auto.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_complete_confirm.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_implement_auto.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_implement_confirm.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_plan_auto.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_plan_confirm.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_resume_auto.yaml
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ speckit_resume_confirm.yaml

deep/                 # Deep workflows (research, review, AI council)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ research.md               # /deep:research - Autonomous deep research loop
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ review.md                 # /deep:review - Autonomous code review loop
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ ai-council.md             # /deep:ai-council - Multi-topic deep AI council
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ assets/
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ deep_research_auto.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ deep_research_confirm.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ deep_review_auto.yaml
    Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ deep_review_confirm.yaml
    ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ deep_ai-council_auto.yaml

> Note: `/doctor skill-advisor` previously lived under `spec_kit/`; it is now organized under `.opencode/commands/doctor/` alongside `mcp_install` and `mcp_debug` since it tunes runtime configuration rather than driving the spec workflow.
```

<!-- /ANCHOR:structure -->

---

<!-- ANCHOR:workflow-progression -->
## 4. WORKFLOW PROGRESSION

The typical development lifecycle follows this progression:

```text
/deep:research (optional)
    |
    v
/speckit:plan (create spec folder + plan.md)
    |
    v
phase (optional: decompose into phase children)
    |
    v
/speckit:implement (execute plan.md tasks)
    |
    v
/speckit:resume (continue in a new or interrupted session)
```

The `complete` command combines research, plan, and implement into a single invocation.

### Agent Delegation

| Command | Delegates To |
|---------|-------------|
| plan | Main agent owns planning and may reuse the shared intake contract (`../../skills/system-spec-kit/references/workflows/intake_contract.md`); /deep:research optional |
| implement | @general (code changes), distributed governance for packet docs |
| deep-research | /deep:research (iterative investigation) |
| deep-review | /deep:review (iterative code audit) |
| resume | Loads memory context, continues from last state |
| phase | Main agent creates packet folders, @general runs scripts as needed |
| complete | /deep:research and @general as needed, with the shared intake contract (`../../skills/system-spec-kit/references/workflows/intake_contract.md`) when packet state requires repair |

<!-- /ANCHOR:workflow-progression -->

---

<!-- ANCHOR:execution-modes -->
## 5. EXECUTION MODES

| Mode | Suffix | Behavior |
|------|--------|----------|
| **Auto** | `:auto` | Execute all steps without approval prompts |
| **Confirm** | `:confirm` | Pause at each step and wait for user approval |

The `complete` command supports two additional flags:

| Flag | Effect |
|------|--------|
| `:with-research` | Add research phase before planning |

Each mode maps to a YAML workflow file in `assets/`:
- Auto: `spec_kit_<command>_auto.yaml`
- Confirm: `spec_kit_<command>_confirm.yaml`

<!-- /ANCHOR:execution-modes -->

---

<!-- ANCHOR:usage-examples -->
## 6. USAGE EXAMPLES

```bash
# Plan a new feature (creates spec folder + plan.md)
/speckit:plan "Add rate limiting to API" :auto

# Implement from an existing plan
/speckit:implement specs/012-rate-limiting :confirm

# Deep research a topic before planning
/deep:research "OAuth 2.0 token refresh patterns" :auto

# Decompose a complex feature into phases
/speckit:plan:auto "Build hybrid RAG search system" :with-phases --phases 3

# Save continuity before ending a long session
/memory:save specs/012-rate-limiting

# Resume work in a new or interrupted session
/speckit:resume specs/012-rate-limiting :auto

13: // ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
14: // 1. TYPES
15: // ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
16: 
17: export interface PropagateEnhancesArgs {
18:   skillsRoot?: string;
19:   mode?: 'report' | 'propose' | 'apply';
20:   minConfidence?: number;
21:   targetSkillIds?: string[];
22:   sourceSkillIds?: string[];
23:   applyCandidateIds?: string[];
24:   applyAllHighConfidence?: boolean;
25:   dryRun?: boolean;
26: }
27: 
28: type HandlerResponse = { content: Array<{ type: string; text: string }> };
29: 
30: // ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
31: // 2. HANDLER
32: // ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
33: 
34: /** Handle skill_graph_propagate_enhances tool call */
35: export async function handleSkillGraphPropagateEnhances(
36:   args: PropagateEnhancesArgs,
37:   callerContext?: MCPCallerContext | null,
38: ): Promise<HandlerResponse> {
39:   try {
40:     // Trust gates the MUTATING path only: report/propose and dry-run apply
41:     // are read-safe and stay open to untrusted callers. This mirrors the
42:     // CLI's documented predicate (real apply = mode apply with dryRun not
43:     // true) exactly, so the two independent enforcement points agree.
44:     const mode = args.mode ?? 'report';
45:     if (mode === 'apply' && args.dryRun !== true) {
46:       const trustedCaller = requireTrustedCaller(callerContext, 'skill_graph_propagate_enhances (mode=apply)');
47:       if (!trustedCaller.ok) {
48:         return errorResponse(trustedCaller.error, trustedCaller.code);
49:       }
50:     }
51: 
52:     const cwd = process.cwd();
53:     const skillsRoot = resolve(cwd, args.skillsRoot ?? '.opencode/skills');
54: 
55:     // Workspace escape guard: resolved path must stay under cwd
56:     if (!skillsRoot.startsWith(cwd + '/') && skillsRoot !== cwd) {
57:       return errorResponse(
58:         `Refusing to scan outside workspace: ${redactDiagnosticText(skillsRoot)} is not under ${redactDiagnosticText(cwd)}`,
59:       );
60:     }
61: 
62:     const result = await propagateInboundEnhances({
63:       skillsRoot,
64:       mode: args.mode ?? 'report',
65:       minConfidence: args.minConfidence,
66:       targetSkillIds: args.targetSkillIds,
67:       sourceSkillIds: args.sourceSkillIds,
68:       applyCandidateIds: args.applyCandidateIds,
69:       applyAllHighConfidence: args.applyAllHighConfidence,
70:       dryRun: args.dryRun,
71:       writeIntent: 'automated',
72:     });
73: 
74:     return okResponse({
75:       skillsRoot,
76:       ...result,
77:     });
78:   } catch (err: unknown) {
79:     return errorResponse(
80:       `Skill graph propagate enhances failed: ${redactDiagnosticText(err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err))}`,
81:     );
82:   }
83: }

170: | `SPECKIT_MAX_SECONDARY_CLIENTS` | `64` | number | Maximum concurrent secondary stdio clients accepted by the daemon IPC socket before new bridge connections are refused. Each live session's launcher holds one persistent slot, and a refused connection (accept-then-close) is indistinguishable from a dead daemon to probes, so keep this above the realistic concurrent-session fleet. Pinned to `64` in all three runtime configs. | `mcp_server/lib/ipc/socket-server.ts` |
171: | `SPECKIT_LAUNCHER_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MIN` | `30` | number | Idle self-exit timeout, in minutes, for the spec-memory, skill-advisor, and code-graph MCP server processes. 12: # Hooks
13: 
14: ---
15: 
16: ## 1. OVERVIEW
17: 
18: `hooks/` contains runtime hook scripts and in-process helper modules for startup context, prompt-time skill advice, compaction support, memory surfacing, mutation feedback, and response hints.
19: 
20: Current state:
21: 
22: - Runtime startup hooks transport compact startup context for Claude and Codex.
23: - Prompt-time hooks call the native advisor and inject a brief through each runtime surface.
24: - `index.ts` exports helper modules used inside MCP responses and mutation flows.
25: - OpenCode prompt-time advice is delivered by the OpenCode plugin and bridge, not by a subfolder in this directory.

# Hooks

---

## 1. OVERVIEW

`hooks/` contains runtime hook scripts and in-process helper modules for startup context, prompt-time skill advice, compaction support, memory surfacing, mutation feedback, and response hints.

Current state:

- Runtime startup hooks transport compact startup context for Claude and Codex.
- Prompt-time hooks call the native advisor and inject a brief through each runtime surface.
- `index.ts` exports helper modules used inside MCP responses and mutation flows.
- OpenCode prompt-time advice is delivered by the OpenCode plugin and bridge, not by a subfolder in this directory.

---

1507: /** Handle memory_index_scan tool ŌĆö dispatches to a synchronous run or a background job.
1508:  *  Without background the call is byte-for-byte identical to the prior synchronous path. */
1509: async function handleMemoryIndexScan(args: ScanArgs): Promise<MCPResponse> {
1510:   if (!args.background) {
1511:     return runIndexScan(args, {});
1512:   }
1513: 
1514:   const jobId = createJobId();
1515:   await createMaintenanceJob({ id: jobId, kind: 'index_scan', payload: redactScanArgs(args) });
1516: 
1517:   setImmediate(() => {
1518:     void (async () => {
1519:       // Mark the daemon busy-by-design so a competing launcher adopts it rather
1520:       // than reaping it mid-scan. The shared marker is reference-counted, so the
1521:       // background-embedding queue draining this scan's deferred embeddings can
1522:       // keep it held past the scan itself.
1523:       const maintenance = beginMaintenance('index_scan');
1524:       try {
1525:         await setJobState(jobId, 'running');
1526:         const response = await runIndexScan(args, {
1527:           isCancelled: () => isCancelRequestedFast(jobId),
1528:           onPhase: (phase) => {
1529:             // Refresh at each phase boundary so a long synchronous tail phase (which
1530:             // cannot fire the interval timer) still enters with a full TTL ahead.
1531:             maintenance.refresh();
1532:             void setJobPhase(jobId, phase).catch(() => {});
1533:           },
1534:           onProgress: (progress) => { void setJobProgress(jobId, progress).catch(() => {}); },
1535:         });
1536:         const scanData = extractEnvelopeData(response);
1537:         const wasCancelled = !!(scanData && typeof scanData === 'object'
1538:           && (scanData as { cancelled?: unknown }).cancelled === true);
1539:         if (wasCancelled) {
1540:           // The run itself short-circuited on cancellation. A cancel that arrives
1541:           // after the scan already finished does not retroactively cancel it.
1542:           await completeJob(jobId, { state: 'cancelled', result: scanData });
1543:         } else if (response.isError === true) {
1544:           // An error envelope (not a thrown error) still means the scan failed.
1545:           await appendJobError(jobId, '__scan__', scanData);
1546:           await setJobState(jobId, 'failed');
1547:         } else {
1548:           await completeJob(jobId, { state: 'complete', result: scanData });
1549:         }
1550:       } catch (error: unknown) {
1551:         try {
1552:           await appendJobError(jobId, '__job__', error);
1553:           await setJobState(jobId, 'failed');
1554:         } catch (_secondaryError: unknown) {
1555:           // Best-effort failure record; never surface a background unhandled rejection.
1556:         }
1557:       } finally {
1558:         // Covers every terminal exit (complete / cancelled / failed / thrown).
1559:         // Release this scan's hold; the marker stays present only while the
1560:         // background-embedding queue still holds it.
1561:         maintenance.end();
1562:       }
1563:     })();
1564:   });
1565: 
1566:   return createMCPSuccessResponse({
1567:     tool: 'memory_index_scan',
1568:     summary: `Queued background index scan ${jobId}`,
1569:     data: { jobId, state: 'queued', background: true },
1570:     hints: [
1571:       'Use memory_index_scan_status with jobId to poll progress',
1572:       'Use memory_index_scan_cancel with jobId to stop the scan',
1573:     ],
1574:   });
1575: }
1576:

198: // Daemon re-election (on by default; set SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION=0 or off to restore kill-on-disposal).
199: // When enabled the owner spawns the daemon detached and, on its own shutdown, RELEASES the daemon
200: // (leaves it running for a live secondary to bridge to) instead of killing it, so a session ending does
201: // not tear the shared backend out from under other sessions. A fresh session that finds the released
202: // daemon under a stale lease ADOPTS it through the bridge when the recorded child is alive and bridgeable;
203: // it reaps and respawns only when that daemon is dead or unbridgeable, so the database keeps a single
204: // writer either way. Disabling collapses every path below to the prior behavior (daemon tied to the owner,
205: // killed on shutdown). A released daemon reparents to pid 1, bounded by its idle self-exit, so the orphan
206: // sweeper bounds any leak.
207: function daemonReelectionEnabled(env = process.env) {
208:   return env.SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION !== '0' && env.SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION !== 'off';

211:   return reelectionEnabled
212:     ? { detached: true, stdio: ['ignore', 'ignore', 'ignore'] }

214: }

1: ---
2: description: Planning workflow (8 steps): spec through plan only. Modes :auto, :confirm, :with-context, :with-phases.
3: argument-hint: "<feature-description> [:auto|:confirm] [:with-context] [:with-phases] [--intake-only] [--phases N] [--phase-names list] [--phase-folder=<path>] [--spec-folder=PATH] [--level=1|2|3|3+] [--start-state=STATE] [--repair-mode=MODE] [--record-relationships=yes|no] [--depends-on=IDs] [--related-to=IDs] [--supersedes=IDs] (:auto supports PRE-BOUND SETUP ANSWERS: prompt-body block for non-interactive setup)"
4: allowed-tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, Task, mcp__mk_spec_memory__memory_context, mcp__mk_spec_memory__memory_search, mcp__mk_spec_memory__memory_save, mcp__mk_spec_memory__memory_index_scan
5: ---
6: 
7: # SpecKit Plan
8: 
9: Thin router for the SpecKit planning workflow. This command resolves the execution mode, loads the presentation contract, then executes the owned workflow YAML.
10: 
11: ## 1. ROUTER CONTRACT
12: 
13: Do not dispatch agents from this Markdown file. Agent dispatch, workflow steps, and artifact-writing behavior are owned by the workflow YAML assets.
14: 
15: Load the presentation contract before showing startup questions, checkpoints, dashboards, success output, failure output, or next-step prompts.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 2. OWNED ASSETS
20: 
21: | Purpose | Asset |
22: |---------|-------|
23: | Presentation source of truth | `.opencode/commands/speckit/assets/speckit_plan_presentation.txt` |
24: | Auto workflow | `.opencode/commands/speckit/assets/speckit_plan_auto.yaml` |
25: | Confirm workflow | `.opencode/commands/speckit/assets/speckit_plan_confirm.yaml` |
26: 
27: No workflow-asset gap exists for this command.
28: 
29: ---
30: 
31: ## 3. MODE ROUTING
32: 
33: 1. Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for `:auto` or `:confirm`.
34: 2. Treat `:with-context`, `:with-phases`, `--intake-only`, `--phases`, `--phase-names`, `--phase-folder`, `--spec-folder`, `--level`, `--start-state`, `--repair-mode`, and relationship flags as workflow inputs, not execution modes.
35: 3. If no mode suffix is present, use the presentation contract's startup prompt to ask for execution mode.
36: 4. For `:auto`, resolve required setup inputs using the presentation contract's auto-resolution rules before loading YAML.
37: 5. Load the selected workflow asset and execute it step by step.
38:

3. If `:auto` is present, set `execution_mode = AUTONOMOUS` and resolve required setup inputs through the presentation contract's three-tier auto setup resolution before loading YAML.

## 6. WORKFLOW SUMMARY

The YAML workflow runs iterative codebase-context gathering by seeding a `SLICE` frontier from the scope, dispatching every executor over the same focus as a parallel sweep, merging findings by `file:symbol` with agreement counts, and synthesizing a reuse-first Context Report under `context/`. Convergence uses deep-context's relevance-gated coverage saturation with agreement and relevance as blocking guards; do not transfer sibling defaults from deep-research or deep-review. Code graph tool IDs remain stable as `code_graph_query` and `code_graph_context`; implementation and docs live under `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/`.

**Context path:** `/deep:context` -> `/speckit:plan` -> `/speckit:implement`
**Research:** `/deep:research` -> `/speckit:plan` -> `/speckit:implement`
**Review:** `/deep:review` -> (if issues) `/speckit:plan` -> `/speckit:implement`

  Line 708: In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, rejects overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. The handler returns an E429 response with the computed wait time whenever either guard rejects the request. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time.

  Line 718: **Phase 013 self-maintaining additions (026):** Overlapping scan requests no longer surface a raw E429 error. The handler detects an active lease and returns a `coalesced: true` success envelope with the computed wait time, so callers can treat concurrent scans as success. Lexical-first async commit means a newly indexed row is BM25/FTS5-searchable immediately with `embedding_status: 'pending'` while its vector drains in the background. The response envelope reports `status: 'complete_with_pending_vectors'` alongside a `pendingVectors` count. Move reconciliation heals renamed spec folders by matching on packet identity (`specId` / folder slug) without re-embedding any content. ### Error code reference (E429, -32001, -32002)

#### Description

Three error codes from different subsystems are easy to confuse. This unifies them into one reference so the call site can answer the only question that matters: is this safe to retry?

#### How It Works

`E429` (`RATE_LIMITED`) is the `memory_index_scan` lease/cooldown rejection; it carries the wait time and a `reason` of `lease_active` or `cooldown`, and is retryable after the wait. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.

#### Source Files

See [`08--bug-fixes-and-data-integrity/error-code-reference.md`](08--bug-fixes-and-data-integrity/error-code-reference.md) for full implementation and test file listings.

-In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, rejects overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. The handler returns an E429 response with the computed wait time whenever either guard rejects the request. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time.

+In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, coalesces overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. Lease-active and cooldown guards now return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, the computed wait time, and polling hints instead of a raw E429. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time. The public scan schema also exposes `background: true`; queued scans can be observed with `memory_index_scan_status` and cancelled with `memory_index_scan_cancel`.
 
 Each file that passes through to indexing goes through the full `memory_save` pipeline, which means content normalization, quality gating, reconsolidation, chunk thinning and encoding-intent capture all apply automatically. Large files are split into chunks, and anchor-aware chunk thinning drops low-scoring chunks before they enter the index.
 
 After indexing, the tool does more than store data. It creates causal chain edges between spec documents within the same folder (spec leads to plan, plan leads to tasks, tasks lead to checklist, and so on). It detects alias conflicts. It runs divergence reconciliation hooks. It clears the trigger matcher cache if any changes occurred.

 Three error codes from different subsystems are easy to confuse. This unifies them into one reference so the call site can answer the only question that matters: is this safe to retry?
 
 #### How It Works
 
-`E429` (`RATE_LIMITED`) is the `memory_index_scan` lease/cooldown rejection; it carries the wait time and a `reason` of `lease_active` or `cooldown`, and is retryable after the wait. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.
+`memory_index_scan` lease-active and cooldown cases no longer surface `E429`; they return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, a wait time, and `reason: 'lease_active'` or `reason: 'cooldown'`. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.

 ### Daemon ownership re-election (default-on)
 
 #### Description
 
-The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is default-on in the committed runtime configs.
+The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is still default-on, but the launcher code is now the source of that default; runtime configs no longer set `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION`.
 
 #### How It Works
 
 When `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION` is on (the launcher code default; set `0` or `off` to disable), the owner spawns the daemon detached and, on shutdown, releases it (keeps the daemon lease, drops only the owner lease, detaches the exit handler so it does not wipe the lease) so a connected live secondary keeps MCP transport. A fresh session that finds the released daemon under the now-stale owner lease reaps the recorded child before respawn, so the database keeps a single writer and a cold restart matches the prior behavior. An unadopted released daemon is bounded by its idle self-exit. Validated by the hermetic release-vs-kill test and the live two-session adoption test `daemon-reelection-adoption-live.vitest.ts`.

The `SPECKIT_CROSS_ENCODER`/`RERANKER_LOCAL` flags are no longer wired.
 
 ## Security
 
 - `VOYAGE_API_KEY` is read from the process environment only. It must never be logged, written into spec docs, or persisted to disk by Spec Kit. Operators should set it in shell init files owned by the operator with mode `600`.
 - Tests may mutate env vars, but must restore them in `afterEach`. Production code paths should not treat mutable process env as request-time configuration.

-Run `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh <spec-folder> --strict` before completion claims. Validation errors block completion; warnings must be addressed or documented. Startup, resume, hook, code graph, and Code Graph readiness details live in `references/config/hook_system.md`, `references/hooks/skill_advisor_hook.md`, `mcp_server/hooks/copilot/README.md`, and the code graph references.

 ### Code Graph and Search Routing
 
 Use Grep/Glob for semantic/token discovery, Code Graph for structural relationships, and Spec Kit Memory for prior decisions and continuity. The `system-code-graph` skill owns the full `mcp__mk_code_index__*` family split: `code_graph_status` is always answerable, `code_graph_classify_query_intent` is text-only, read tools (`code_graph_query`, `code_graph_context`, `detect_changes`) return blocked/degraded payloads under the readiness contract, and maintenance tools (`code_graph_scan`, `code_graph_apply`, `code_graph_verify`) handle recovery and verification, with verify blocking when graph state is not fresh.

Index: /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md
===================================================================
--- /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md
+++ /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/feature_catalog/feature_catalog.md
@@ -704,9 +704,9 @@
 Spec documents are still indexed by default. During scan they flow through `memory_save` with `qualityGateMode: 'warn-only'`, so template, sufficiency, and quality issues surface as warnings instead of silently bypassing retrieval.
 
 The scanner discovers files from three sources: constitutional files (`.opencode/skills/*/constitutional/*.md`), canonical spec documents (`spec.md`, `plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `checklist.md`, `decision-record.md`, `implementation-summary.md`, `research.md`, `research/research.md`, `resource-map.md`, `handover.md`, root-level `review-report.md`, `<packet>/review/review-report.md`, and `description.json`), and graph metadata files (`graph-metadata.json`). Metadata files backfilled under `<packet>/iterations/` are discoverable, while `research/iterations/` and `review/iterations/` markdown remain working artifacts. The retired `specs/**/memory/*.md` surface is no longer discovered and the runtime rejects saves into it. Canonical path deduplication prevents the same file from being indexed twice under different paths (the `specs/` vs `.opencode/specs/` symlink problem).
 
-In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, rejects overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. The handler returns an E429 response with the computed wait time whenever either guard rejects the request. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time.
+In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, coalesces overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. Lease-active and cooldown guards now return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, the computed wait time, and polling hints instead of a raw E429. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time. The public scan schema also exposes `background: true`; queued scans can be observed with `memory_index_scan_status` and cancelled with `memory_index_scan_cancel`.
 
 Each file that passes through to indexing goes through the full `memory_save` pipeline, which means content normalization, quality gating, reconsolidation, chunk thinning and encoding-intent capture all apply automatically. Large files are split into chunks, and anchor-aware chunk thinning drops low-scoring chunks before they enter the index.
 
 After indexing, the tool does more than store data. It creates causal chain edges between spec documents within the same folder (spec leads to plan, plan leads to tasks, tasks lead to checklist, and so on). It detects alias conflicts. It runs divergence reconciliation hooks. It clears the trigger matcher cache if any changes occurred.

 Three error codes from different subsystems are easy to confuse. This unifies them into one reference so the call site can answer the only question that matters: is this safe to retry?
 
 #### How It Works
 
-`E429` (`RATE_LIMITED`) is the `memory_index_scan` lease/cooldown rejection; it carries the wait time and a `reason` of `lease_active` or `cooldown`, and is retryable after the wait. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.
+`memory_index_scan` lease-active and cooldown cases no longer surface `E429`; they return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, a wait time, and `reason: 'lease_active'` or `reason: 'cooldown'`. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.
 
 #### Source Files
 
 See [`08--bug-fixes-and-data-integrity/error-code-reference.md`](08--bug-fixes-and-data-integrity/error-code-reference.md) for full implementation and test file listings.
@@ -3613,9 +3613,9 @@
 ### Daemon ownership re-election (default-on)
 
 #### Description
 
-The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is default-on in the committed runtime configs.
+The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is still default-on, but the launcher code is now the source of that default; runtime configs no longer set `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION`.
 
 #### How It Works
 
 When `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION` is on (the launcher code default; set `0` or `off` to disable), the owner spawns the daemon detached and, on shutdown, releases it (keeps the daemon lease, drops only the owner lease, detaches the exit handler so it does not wipe the lease) so a connected live secondary keeps MCP transport. A fresh session that finds the released daemon under the now-stale owner lease reaps the recorded child before respawn, so the database keeps a single writer and a cold restart matches the prior behavior. An unadopted released daemon is bounded by its idle self-exit. Validated by the hermetic release-vs-kill test and the live two-session adoption test `daemon-reelection-adoption-live.vitest.ts`.

<content>
419: The current memory baseline is schema v37. The 027 hardening features ship behind conservative defaults: semantic-trigger shadow matching, session-trace causal inference, feedback-retention reducers, soft-delete tombstones, memory idempotency receipts, authored continuity snapshots, and completion freshness all stay opt-in. `source_kind` provenance, retrieval observability, stale-audit signals, and tool-ownership linting are documented in the memory and ENV references rather than duplicated here.
420: 
421: ### Reranking
422: 
423: Model-based cross-encoder/local-GGUF reranking was removed in the 014 deprecation: the spec-memory local model path was removed in phase 003 and the local rerank sidecar skill was deleted in phase 004 (cloud rerankers were removed earlier in 022/013). Memory search still has a Stage 3 rerank step: MMR diversity reranking plus MPAB chunk collapse, with the `memory_search` `rerank` option defaulting to true. The `SPECKIT_CROSS_ENCODER`/`RERANKER_LOCAL` flags are no longer wired.
424: 
425: ## Security
426: 
427: - `VOYAGE_API_KEY` is read from the process environment only. It must never be logged, written into spec docs, or persisted to disk by Spec Kit. Operators should set it in shell init files owned by the operator with mode `600`.
428: - Tests may mutate env vars, but must restore them in `afterEach`. Production code paths should not treat mutable process env as request-time configuration.
429: 
430: ### Validation and Recovery
431: 
432: Run `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh <spec-folder> --strict` before completion claims. Validation errors block completion; warnings must be addressed or documented. Startup, resume, hook, code graph, and Code Graph readiness details live in `references/config/hook_system.md`, `references/hooks/skill_advisor_hook.md`, `mcp_server/hooks/README.md` (Claude and Codex hook folders; OpenCode uses the plugin bridge), and the code graph references.

metadata.preview:
The current memory baseline is schema v37. The 027 hardening features ship behind conservative defaults: semantic-trigger shadow matching, session-trace causal inference, feedback-retention reducers, soft-delete tombstones, memory idempotency receipts, authored continuity snapshots, and completion freshness all stay opt-in. `source_kind` provenance, retrieval observability, stale-audit signals, and tool-ownership linting are documented in the memory and ENV references rather than duplicated here.

### Reranking

Model-based cross-encoder/local-GGUF reranking was removed in the 014 deprecation: the spec-memory local model path was removed in phase 003 and the local rerank sidecar skill was deleted in phase 004 (cloud rerankers were removed earlier in 022/013). Memory search still has a Stage 3 rerank step: MMR diversity reranking plus MPAB chunk collapse, with the `memory_search` `rerank` option defaulting to true. The `SPECKIT_CROSS_ENCODER`/`RERANKER_LOCAL` flags are no longer wired.

## Security

- `VOYAGE_API_KEY` is read from the process environment only. It must never be logged, written into spec docs, or persisted to disk by Spec Kit. Operators should set it in shell init files owned by the operator with mode `600`.
- Tests may mutate env vars, but must restore them in `afterEach`. Production code paths should not treat mutable process env as request-time configuration.

### Validation and Recovery

Run `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/spec/validate.sh <spec-folder> --strict` before completion claims. Validation errors block completion; warnings must be addressed or documented. Startup, resume, hook, code graph, and Code Graph readiness details live in `references/config/hook_system.md`, `references/hooks/skill_advisor_hook.md`, `mcp_server/hooks/README.md` (Claude and Codex hook folders; OpenCode uses the plugin bridge), and the code graph references.

metadata.preview:
### Error code reference (E429, -32001, -32002)

#### Description

Three error codes from different subsystems are easy to confuse. This unifies them into one reference so the call site can answer the only question that matters: is this safe to retry?

#### How It Works

`memory_index_scan` lease-active and cooldown cases no longer surface `E429`; they return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, a wait time, and `reason: 'lease_active'` or `reason: 'cooldown'`. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.

display.text:
### Error code reference (E429, -32001, -32002)

#### Description

Three error codes from different subsystems are easy to confuse. This unifies them into one reference so the call site can answer the only question that matters: is this safe to retry?

#### How It Works

`memory_index_scan` lease-active and cooldown cases no longer surface `E429`; they return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, a wait time, and `reason: 'lease_active'` or `reason: 'cooldown'`. `-32001` (`RETRYABLE_RECYCLE_ERROR`, `{ retryable: true }`) is the launcher proxy's still-live "backend recycled; retry" signal for in-flight non-replayable requests during an in-place daemon recycle ŌĆö it is NOT removed; only the unrelated index vector-drain outage path stopped surfacing its own `-32001` class. `-32002` (`PROTOCOL_MISMATCH_ERROR`, `{ retryable: false }`) is emitted when a re-handshaked backend negotiates a different protocol version; the proxy fails closed to a terminal `CLOSED` state and the client must reconnect from scratch.

3613: ### Daemon ownership re-election (default-on)
3614: 
3615: #### Description
3616: 
3617: The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is still default-on, but the launcher code is now the source of that default; runtime configs no longer set `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION`.
3618: 
3619: #### How It Works
3620: 
3621: When `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION` is on (the launcher code default; set `0` or `off` to disable), the owner spawns the daemon detached and, on shutdown, releases it (keeps the daemon lease, drops only the owner lease, detaches the exit handler so it does not wipe the lease) so a connected live secondary keeps MCP transport. A fresh session that finds the released daemon under the now-stale owner lease reaps the recorded child before respawn, so the database keeps a single writer and a cold restart matches the prior behavior. An unadopted released daemon is bounded by its idle self-exit. Validated by the hermetic release-vs-kill test and the live two-session adoption test `daemon-reelection-adoption-live.vitest.ts`.

metadata.preview:
### Daemon ownership re-election (default-on)

#### Description

The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is still default-on, but the launcher code is now the source of that default; runtime configs no longer set `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION`.

#### How It Works

When `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION` is on (the launcher code default; set `0` or `off` to disable), the owner spawns the daemon detached and, on shutdown, releases it (keeps the daemon lease, drops only the owner lease, detaches the exit handler so it does not wipe the lease) so a connected live secondary keeps MCP transport. A fresh session that finds the released daemon under the now-stale owner lease reaps the recorded child before respawn, so the database keeps a single writer and a cold restart matches the prior behavior. An unadopted released daemon is bounded by its idle self-exit. Validated by the hermetic release-vs-kill test and the live two-session adoption test `daemon-reelection-adoption-live.vitest.ts`.

display.text:
### Daemon ownership re-election (default-on)

#### Description

The shared daemon used to die with its owner because the owner killed it on shutdown. Re-election lets the daemon outlive its owner so secondary sessions keep the backend. It is still default-on, but the launcher code is now the source of that default; runtime configs no longer set `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION`.

#### How It Works

When `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION` is on (the launcher code default; set `0` or `off` to disable), the owner spawns the daemon detached and, on shutdown, releases it (keeps the daemon lease, drops only the owner lease, detaches the exit handler so it does not wipe the lease) so a connected live secondary keeps MCP transport. A fresh session that finds the released daemon under the now-stale owner lease reaps the recorded child before respawn, so the database keeps a single writer and a cold restart matches the prior behavior. An unadopted released daemon is bounded by its idle self-exit. Validated by the hermetic release-vs-kill test and the live two-session adoption test `daemon-reelection-adoption-live.vitest.ts`.

5122: #### How It Works
5123: 
5124: | Name | Default | Type | Source File | Description |
5125: |---|---|---|---|---|
5126: | `EMBEDDING_DIM` | _(provider default)_ | number | `lib/search/vector-index-store.ts`, `shared/embeddings/factory.ts` | Compatibility check and startup override for the stored vector dimension. Any positive explicit `EMBEDDING_DIM` value is honored first; otherwise runtime dimension selection comes from the active provider profile (Voyage 1024, OpenAI 1536, local nomic 768). |
5127: | `EMBEDDINGS_PROVIDER` | `'auto'` | string | `shared/embeddings/factory.ts` | Selects the embedding provider. Valid values include `'auto'`, `'openai'`, `'hf-local'`, `'ollama'`, and `'voyage'`. Provider resolution is local-first: an explicit non-auto `EMBEDDINGS_PROVIDER` is the only direct cloud selector, then auto mode uses persisted/reachable Ollama, then `hf-local`. 704: Spec documents are still indexed by default. During scan they flow through `memory_save` with `qualityGateMode: 'warn-only'`, so template, sufficiency, and quality issues surface as warnings instead of silently bypassing retrieval.
705: 
706: The scanner discovers files from three sources: constitutional files (`.opencode/skills/*/constitutional/*.md`), canonical spec documents (`spec.md`, `plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `checklist.md`, `decision-record.md`, `implementation-summary.md`, `research.md`, `research/research.md`, `resource-map.md`, `handover.md`, root-level `review-report.md`, `<packet>/review/review-report.md`, and `description.json`), and graph metadata files (`graph-metadata.json`). Metadata files backfilled under `<packet>/iterations/` are discoverable, while `research/iterations/` and `review/iterations/` markdown remain working artifacts. The retired `specs/**/memory/*.md` surface is no longer discovered and the runtime rejects saves into it. Canonical path deduplication prevents the same file from being indexed twice under different paths (the `specs/` vs `.opencode/specs/` symlink problem).
707: 
708: In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, coalesces overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. Lease-active and cooldown guards now return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, the computed wait time, and polling hints instead of a raw E429. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time. The public scan schema also exposes `background: true`; queued scans can be observed with `memory_index_scan_status` and cancelled with `memory_index_scan_cancel`.
709: 
710: Each file that passes through to indexing goes through the full `memory_save` pipeline, which means content normalization, quality gating, reconsolidation, chunk thinning and encoding-intent capture all apply automatically. Large files are split into chunks, and anchor-aware chunk thinning drops low-scoring chunks before they enter the index.

metadata.preview:
Spec documents are still indexed by default. During scan they flow through `memory_save` with `qualityGateMode: 'warn-only'`, so template, sufficiency, and quality issues surface as warnings instead of silently bypassing retrieval.

The scanner discovers files from three sources: constitutional files (`.opencode/skills/*/constitutional/*.md`), canonical spec documents (`spec.md`, `plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `checklist.md`, `decision-record.md`, `implementation-summary.md`, `research.md`, `research/research.md`, `resource-map.md`, `handover.md`, root-level `review-report.md`, `<packet>/review/review-report.md`, and `description.json`), and graph metadata files (`graph-metadata.json`). Metadata files backfilled under `<packet>/iterations/` are discoverable, while `research/iterations/` and `review/iterations/` markdown remain working artifacts. The retired `specs/**/memory/*.md` surface is no longer discovered and the runtime rejects saves into it. Canonical path deduplication prevents the same file from being indexed twice under different paths (the `specs/` vs `.opencode/specs/` symlink problem).

In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, coalesces overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. Lease-active and cooldown guards now return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, the computed wait time, and polling hints instead of a raw E429. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time. The public scan schema also exposes `background: true`; queued scans can be observed with `memory_index_scan_status` and cancelled with `memory_index_scan_cancel`.

Each file that passes through to indexing goes through the full `memory_save` pipeline, which means content normalization, quality gating, reconsolidation, chunk thinning and encoding-intent capture all apply automatically. Large files are split into chunks, and anchor-aware chunk thinning drops low-scoring chunks before they enter the index.

display.text:
Spec documents are still indexed by default. During scan they flow through `memory_save` with `qualityGateMode: 'warn-only'`, so template, sufficiency, and quality issues surface as warnings instead of silently bypassing retrieval.

The scanner discovers files from three sources: constitutional files (`.opencode/skills/*/constitutional/*.md`), canonical spec documents (`spec.md`, `plan.md`, `tasks.md`, `checklist.md`, `decision-record.md`, `implementation-summary.md`, `research.md`, `research/research.md`, `resource-map.md`, `handover.md`, root-level `review-report.md`, `<packet>/review/review-report.md`, and `description.json`), and graph metadata files (`graph-metadata.json`). Metadata files backfilled under `<packet>/iterations/` are discoverable, while `research/iterations/` and `review/iterations/` markdown remain working artifacts. The retired `specs/**/memory/*.md` surface is no longer discovered and the runtime rejects saves into it. Canonical path deduplication prevents the same file from being indexed twice under different paths (the `specs/` vs `.opencode/specs/` symlink problem).

In incremental mode (the default), the scanner categorizes every discovered file into one of four buckets: to-index (new files), to-update (changed mtime or content hash), to-skip (unchanged mtime and matching content hash) and to-delete (files that disappeared from disk). The content-hash secondary check catches timestamp-preserving rewrites that would otherwise look unchanged from mtime alone. Batch processing with a configurable `BATCH_SIZE` handles large workspaces, but oversized requests are clamped to the hard runtime maximum with a warning instead of exploding fan-out. Scan throttling uses an atomic lease: `acquireIndexScanLease()` reads `last_index_scan` and `scan_started_at` in one transaction, expires stale leases left behind by crashed scans, reserves a fresh run by writing `scan_started_at`, coalesces overlapping fresh scans with `reason: 'lease_active'`, and still enforces `INDEX_SCAN_COOLDOWN` after completed runs with `reason: 'cooldown'`. Lease-active and cooldown guards now return a success envelope with `coalesced: true`, `status: 'coalesced'`, the computed wait time, and polling hints instead of a raw E429. `completeIndexScanLease()` runs after the scan response is assembled, converts the active lease into `last_index_scan`, and clears the active lease row so the cooldown clock ties to completed scans instead of request start time. The public scan schema also exposes `background: true`; queued scans can be observed with `memory_index_scan_status` and cancelled with `memory_index_scan_cancel`.

Each file that passes through to indexing goes through the full `memory_save` pipeline, which means content normalization, quality gating, reconsolidation, chunk thinning and encoding-intent capture all apply automatically. Large files are split into chunks, and anchor-aware chunk thinning drops low-scoring chunks before they enter the index.

/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/SKILL.md:
  Line 413: The surface is dual-stack: alongside the `mk-spec-memory` MCP registration, all 39 tools are callable through the full-parity daemon-backed CLI `node .opencode/bin/spec-memory.cjs <tool_name> [--json '{...}' | --param value]` against the same daemon. MCP remains the primary in-session transport today; use the CLI when MCP transport is missing, failed or not reconnecting while the daemon is warm, and for hooks, cron, CI and operator shell diagnostics. Recovery example: `node .opencode/bin/spec-memory.cjs memory_context --json '{"input":"resume previous work","mode":"resume"}' --format json --timeout-ms 3000`. CLI exit taxonomy: `0` success, `1` runtime, `64` usage/schema, `69` protocol/dist mismatch or stale dist, `75` retryable daemon error. Prompt-time callers must pass `--warm-only` (probe-only, exit `75` instead of cold-spawning); non-prompt contexts auto-spawn the daemon through the launcher. Because this CLI already has full parity, a later evolution could make it the primary or sole transport without breaking existing MCP workflows; that is a possible direction, not a committed plan. `--format jsonl` renders one complete JSON payload on one stdout line; it is not streaming JSON Lines. Full cross-daemon CLI behavior, recovery, stale-dist build commands, per-command `--help`, offline smoke, and safety rules live in [`references/cli/daemon_cli_reference.md`](references/cli/daemon_cli_reference.md). See `mcp_server/ENV_REFERENCE.md` ("CLI front door") for the warm-only/prompt-time env flags. Detailed behavior, flags, scoring, and MCP tool reference live in `references/memory/memory_system.md`, `references/memory/save_workflow.md`, and `mcp_server/ENV_REFERENCE.md`. Launcher/daemon reliability is operator-tunable via the `SPECKIT_LAUNCHER_LOG`, `SPECKIT_LEASE_PROBE_RETRIES`, `SPECKIT_STOP_HOOK_ORPHAN_SWEEP`, and `SPECKIT_DAEMON_REELECTION` (default-on in the runtime configs: a disposing owner releases the shared daemon for a live secondary, and a fresh session reap...
